course_description:
This course examines the fundamental importance of energy transitions for achieving sustainable development. During the course, students will explore a variety of global change issues including resource depletion, population, consumption, security and conflict and investigate the ensuing implications for energy. With a focus on both the developed, fast developing and developing countries by applying a range of legal, political, regulatory and behavioural frameworks, the course examines mechanisms required to achieve a transition towards a low carbon future.

This course enables students to become familiar with the range of grand challenges currently being faced around the world. The focus is on both the developed and developing world as well as countries in between.ﾠ During the course, students will explore a variety of global change issues and investigate the ensuing implications for energy, resources, population, consumption, security and conflict as well as relations between states.ﾠ Students will be provided with basic facts and the capacity to argue clear cases around mechanisms to achieve a transition towards a low carbon future by applying a range of legal, political, regulatory and behavioural frameworks.

incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:
restrictions: Restricted to MSE, GDSE, GCSE, MEI, GCEI, MEngSc and MEngSc(Man) students.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
This course is taught in Intensive mode.ﾠ Students are advised to check the Learning Activities section for the course timetable.ﾠ In particular, attendance and participation - on campus (recommended) or online - is required at the Intensive Modules across two consecutive days (9am-5pm Brisbane time, each day), as well as 5 x weekly 3-hour workshops.
course_aims: This course aims to provide students with an understanding of key global challenges that may slow or impede the successful transition to a low carbon economy. Using the United Nation’s Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) as basis, the course will examine the implications of these for both developed and developing countries. With a particular focus on energy access (SDG7), the course aims to develop comprehensive knowledge and competence to enable students to critique various development paradigms and drivers of change.ﾠ At a project level, the course gives students the tools to consider stakeholder, cultural and enviornmental implications of energy proposals alongside those of a techno-economic nature.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand and critique the multi-dimensional role of energy in achieving sustainable development goals.
Adopt a systems approach when examining energy access issues such as the energy trilemma.
Critically evaluate development paradigms and the contributions of the public and private sectors in achieving sustainable energy access for all.
Demonstrate an understanding appropriate technology for energy access projects.
Articulate the social and stakeholder impacts of energy proposals and management of social licence to operate.
Reflect on sustainability leadership and working in multi-disciplinary, professional teams.

Individual Project: Energy Trilemma Case Study

assessment_details.task_description: Multiple-choice quiz on Blackboard, to test understanding of Foundation Modules. Via Blackboard.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
Late submission

assessment_details.title: Report: Community Energy Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: Students will work in teams, initially through in-class workshops, to develop a proposed energy solution for a given community. The report should include an assessment of the appropriateness of the chosen technology, proposed business model and the impacts on the SDGs. Refer to Blackboard for more details. This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). assessment_details.title: Individual Project: Energy Trilemma Case Study
assessment_details.task_description: National Case Study (Individual Project) Strategy for improving a nation's Energy Trilemma Index Using the World Energy Council Trilemma Index http://www.worldenergy.org/data/trilemma-index/ you will focus on one nation to become the focus of your case study. The main aim for your work is to propose to the nation's government an intervention that will help to move the nation towards a more positive trend on the Index (i.e. up the ladder and therefore closer to 1). The WEC national report will provide you with a starting point for your work. However, you are expected to research widely to discover more detailed information about the nation. This will include gathering information on current and expected energy demands, existing energy sources, current policy and regulatory framework. You are advised to use case studies from other nations, and academic articles, to help inform your approach. Your submission for the Individual Project consists of: 1. Written Responses to key criteria, using systems diagrams 2. Presentation explainer on connections with SDGs This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Foundation Modules Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Intensive Modules - Days 1 & 2 Intensive modules run 9am-5pm Monday and Tuesday in Week 2. Introduction to course Energy access: affordable, reliable, sustainable energy in the context of development Energy's connections with poverty alleviation Systems thinking and systems dynamics Appropriate Technology Indigenous energy projects Stakeholders Social Licence to Operate Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 3 To Week 7 (10 Mar - 13 Apr)
learning_activities.topic: Workshops 5 x weekly 3-hour workshops, including: Group presentations (assessment) Leadership module and workshop on community energy Systems thinking Sustainable Development Goals Energy and critical minerals Learning outcomes: L03, L04, L05, L06

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
